Netflix=E2=80=99s moment of greatest peril is, in retrospect, barely visibl=
e in the company=E2=80=99s stock chart:

I=E2=80=99m referring to 2004-2007 and the company=E2=80=99s battle with Bl=
ockbuster:

The simplified story of Netflix=E2=80=99s founding starts with Reed Hasting=
s grumbling over a $40 late charge from Blockbuster, and ends with the bric=
k-and-mortar giant going bankrupt as customers came to prefer online rental=
s from Netflix, with streaming providing the final coup de gr=C3=A2ce.

Neither are quite right.

The Blockbuster Fight
---------------------

Netflix was the idea of Marc Randolph, Netflix=E2=80=99s actual founder and=
 first CEO; Randolph was eager to do something in e-commerce, and it was th=
e just-emerging DVD form factor that sold Hastings on the idea. He would fu=
nd Randolph=E2=80=99s new company and be chairman, eventually taking over a=
s CEO once he determined that Randolph was not up to the task of scaling th=
e new company.

Blockbuster, meanwhile, mounted a far more serious challenge to Netflix tha=
n many people remember; the company started with Blockbuster Online, an ent=

hated having an online service cannibalize their sales. Still, when a test =

=99s quarterly earnings call =E2=80=94=C2=A0Netflix=E2=80=99s stock suffere=

Three months later Netflix cut prices and referred to Amazon=E2=80=99s assu=
med imminent entry to the space; Netflix=E2=80=99s stock slid again. Hastin=
gs, though, said the increased competition and looming price war was actual=
ly a good thing. Gina Keating relayed Hastings=E2=80=99 view on that quarte=

books/310598/netflixed-by-gina-keating/9781101601433 ) :

=E2=80=9CLook, everyone, I know the Amazon entry is a bitter and surprising=

ge market, and we=E2=80=99re going to execute very hard to make this back f=
or our shareholders, including ourselves.=E2=80=9D The $8 billion in U.S. s=
tore rentals would pour into online rentals, setting off a grab for subscri=

Blockbuster responded by pricing Blockbuster Online 50 cents cheaper, accel=

Netflix was right:

The flood of marketing dollars that Antioco had committed to Blockbuster On=

Still, Netflix wasn=E2=80=99t yet out of the woods: in 2006 Blockbuster lau=

His momentary and grudging admiration for Antioco for finally figuring out =

From Spock to Kirk
------------------

Reed Hastings has certainly earned the right to step up =E2=80=94 and back =
=E2=80=94 to executive chairman; last Thursday was his last earnings interv=
iew. It=E2=80=99s interesting, though, to go back to his initial move from =
chairman to the CEO role. Randolph writes in the first chapter of his book =
That Will Never Work ( https://www.littlebrown.com/titles/marc-randolph/tha=
t-will-never-work/9780316530200/ ) :

Behind his back, I=E2=80=99ve heard people compare Reed to Spock. I don=E2=
=80=99t think they mean it as a compliment, but they should. In Star Trek, =
Spock is almost always right. And Reed is, too. If he thinks something won=
=E2=80=99t work, it probably won=E2=80=99t.

Unfortunately for Randolph, it didn=E2=80=99t take long for Spock to evalua=
te his performance as CEO; Randolph recounted the conversation:

=E2=80=9CMarc,=E2=80=9D Reed said, =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99re headed for troubl=
e, and I want you to recognize as a shareholder that there is enough smoke =
at this small business size that fire at a larger size is likely. Ours is a=
n execution play. We have to move fast and almost flawlessly. The competiti=
on will be direct and strong. Yahoo! went from a grad school project to a s=
ix-billion-dollar company on awesome execution. We have to do the same thin=
g. I=E2=80=99m not sure we can if you=E2=80=99re the only one in charge.=E2=
=80=9D

He paused, then looked down, as if trying to gain the strength to do someth=
ing difficult. He looked up again, right at me. I remember thinking: He=E2=
=80=99s looking me in the eye. =E2=80=9CSo I think the best possible outcom=
e would be if I joined the company full-time and we ran it together. Me as =
CEO, you as president.=E2=80=9D

Things changed quickly; Keating writes:

Hastings now held Netflix=E2=80=99s reins firmly in hand, and the VC money =
gave him the power to begin shifting the company=E2=80=99s culture away fro=
m Randolph=E2=80=99s family of creators toward a top-down organization led =
by executives with proven corporate records and, preferably, strong enginee=
ring and mathematics backgrounds.

Randolph ultimately left the company in 2002; again from Keating:

The last year or so of Randolph=E2=80=99s career at Netflix was a time of i=
ndecision =E2=80=94 stay or go? He had resigned from the board of directors=
 before the IPO, in part so that investors would not view his desire to cas=
h out some of his equity as a vote of no confidence in the newly public com=
pany. Randolph landed in product development while trying to find a role fo=
r himself at Netflix, and dove into Lowe=E2=80=99s kiosk project and a vide=
o-streaming application that the engineers were beginning to develop. But a=
fter seven years of lavishing time and attention on his start-up, Randolph =
needed a break. Netflix had changed around him, from his collective of drea=
mers trying to change the world into Hastings=E2=80=99 hypercompetitive tea=
m of engineers and right-brained marketers whose skills intimidated him sli=
ghtly. He no longer fit in.

deed, the speed with which the company rolled out its advertising product i=

icularly when Netflix was shipping DVDs: acquiring customers efficiently an=

What is notable, though, is that the chief task for Netflix going forward i=
s not necessarily execution, at least in terms of product or technology. Wh=
ile Hastings has left Netflix in a very good spot relative to its competito=
rs, the long-term success of the company will ultimately be about creativit=
y. Specifically, can Netflix produce compelling content at scale? Matthew B=

Netflix is, in some regard, a sobering story. What do I mean by that? First=
 mover advantages matter a lot, scale matters a lot, their product and tech=
nology investments matter a lot. Reed [Hastings] saw the future for global =
content services and scale that span every market, every genre, every perso=
n, truly years before any competitor did. I think we see pretty intense com=
petition right now, but it=E2=80=99s remarkable when you actually look at t=
he corporate histories of all of the competitors, most have changed leaders=
hip at the CEO level twice, at the DTC level three to four times, Hulu is o=
n its fifth or sixth CEO and so we have to give incredible plaudits to all =
of that.

Yet what I find so important here, is at the end of the day, all of those t=
hings only matter for a while. Content matters, that=E2=80=99s the product =
that they=E2=80=99re selling, it=E2=80=99s entertainment. The thing that ha=
s surprised me most about Netflix is their struggles to get better at it. W=
hen I was at Amazon Studios and we were competing with them day in and day =
out, the assumption you would=E2=80=99ve made in 2015, =E2=80=9916, =E2=80=
=9917 would be that the Netflix of 2022 would be much better at making cont=
ent than it seems to be. That their batting average would be much higher. W=
hy? Because they=E2=80=99ve spent $70 or $80 billion since and I think we=
=E2=80=99re starting to feel the consequences of [not being as far ahead as=
 expected].

It=E2=80=99s impossible to not dive into the history of Netflix and not com=
e away with a deep appreciation for everything Hastings accomplished. I=E2=
=80=99m not sure there is any company of Netflix=E2=80=99s size that has ev=
er been so frequently doubted and written off. To have built it to a state =
where simply having the best content is paramount is a massive triumph. And=
 that, perhaps, is another way of saying that Spock=E2=80=99s job is finish=
ed: Netflix=E2=80=99s future is about creativity and humanity; it=E2=80=99s=
 time for a Captain Kirk.
